text.title: data-analysis Failure Modes
text.summary: Migrated antipatterns for data-analysis as failure modes. Add detector + repair clauses where missing.

failure-mode.description: No data dictionary — everyone defines data differently.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Without a shared data dictionary, different teams build against different definitions of the same entity; the inconsistency surfaces as data quality failures after go-live, not before.

failure-mode.description: Skipping data quality assessment — assuming current data is correct.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Systems built on inaccurate or inconsistent data inherit the data's defects; pre-spec assessment is orders of magnitude cheaper than post-launch remediation.

failure-mode.description: Technical-only data modeling without business definitions.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Column names and data types without business meanings produce a dictionary that developers can implement but stakeholders cannot validate; discrepancies appear only during UAT.

failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Without a named owner per entity, data quality degrades because nobody has authority or accountability to enforce rules or resolve conflicts between source systems.

failure-mode.description: Ignoring historical data — not considering how data will change over time.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Systems designed without archival, versioning, or audit-trail requirements discover these needs at scale after launch, requiring costly schema migrations.
